mission.title: Install and Configure Opengemini on Kubernetes
mission.description: openGemini is a cloud-native distributed time-series database designed for high concurrency, performance, and scalability. Installing it allows you to efficiently store and analyze massive telemetry data in your Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the openGemini Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official openGemini Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add opengemini https://charts.opengemini.org
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install openGemini using Helm
steps.description:
Install openGemini in a new namespace called 'opengemini' with the following command:
```bash
helm install opengemini opengemini/opengemini --namespace opengemini --create-namespace --version v1.5.2
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the openGemini pods are running with the following command:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace opengemini
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the openGemini deployment to set resource limits. Use the following command to open the deployment in your default editor:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment opengemini --namespace opengemini
```
Add the following under the container spec:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
```

steps.title: Port-forward to access openGemini
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to access openGemini from your local machine with the following command:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/opengemini 8080:80 --namespace opengemini
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have openGemini running in the 'opengemini' namespace with its pods in the 'Running' state. You should be able to access it via port 8080 on your local machine.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add opengemini https://charts.opengemini.org
helm repo update
helm install opengemini opengemini/opengemini --namespace opengemini --create-namespace --version v1.5.2

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the openGemini release from the Kubernetes cluster using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall opengemini --namespace opengemini
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with openGemini:
```bash
kubectl delete crd opengemini.*
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace opengemini
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'opengemini' namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace opengemini
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, create a backup of the current release configuration:
```bash
helm get values opengemini --namespace opengemini > opengemini-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the openGemini release to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade opengemini opengemini/opengemini --namespace opengemini --version v1.5.3
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check if the openGemini pods are running the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace opengemini
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the openGemini pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace opengemini
``` 
Look for any error messages that indicate configuration issues or resource limits.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access openGemini through port-forwarding, ensure the service is running:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace opengemini
``` 
If the service is not running, check the deployment status and logs.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are failing due to insufficient resources, check the resource requests and limits:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment opengemini --namespace opengemini
``` 
Adjust the resource limits in the deployment if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating that the Helm release is not found, verify the release name and namespace:
```bash
helm list --namespace opengemini
``` 
Ensure you are using the correct release name 'opengemini'.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster and Helm installed to deploy openGemini.
